answer:
**The maqta (closing stanza) of Allama Iqbal's poem "Dil se jo baat nikalti hai asar rakhti hai" (Bang-E-Dara Part 3, ch. 4) is the final paragraph:**

> Your shield is Reason, Passion is your sword;  
> My humble dervish, yours is the world’s reward:  
> A global caliphate. Your battle cry  
> Is fire for all but God. If you but try  
> To be a Muslim, true in heart and deed,  
> Your fate is your own will, your own creed.  
> Be true to Muhammad, and We are wholly thine;  
> This world is nothing—Pen and Tablet of all Fate are thine.[^1]

This concluding section invokes divine address, Iqbal's vision of Muslim renewal, and fidelity to the Prophet Muhammad, aligning with the poem's themes of spiritual potency and communal awakening (as highlighted in the chapter's wiki card).[^2]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4 "Dil se jo baat nikalti hai asar rakhti hai" (translation), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-819a53).

[^2]: Wiki card for Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4 (closes section), /works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/.
